questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.prompt: Regarding the structural barriers and decision-making powers of the UN Security Council (UNSC), which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: Decisions of the UNSC are recommendatory and require a simple majority of the General Assembly to become binding.
An amendment to the UN Charter requires a two-thirds vote in the General Assembly but does not require ratification by the permanent members.
The UNSC has the power to make binding decisions that all UN member states are obligated to implement under the UN Charter.
India has been elected as a non-permanent member of the UNSC for ten terms since its inception in 1945.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the UNSC is the only UN body with the power to make binding decisions that member states must implement. Option A is incorrect as UNSC decisions are already binding. Option B is incorrect because any amendment to the UN Charter requires ratification by all five permanent members (P5). Option D is incorrect because India has been elected eight times, not ten.
questions.takeaway: UNSC reform is exceptionally difficult because it requires a UN Charter amendment, which necessitates unanimous ratification by the five permanent members.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: UNSC has the power to make binding decisions that member states must implement under the UN Charter... Requires a UN Charter amendment, needing a two-thirds General Assembly vote and ratification by all five permanent members.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.prompt: Which of the following is a mandatory requirement for the formal amendment of the UN Charter to implement UNSC reforms?

options.text: A simple majority vote in the UN General Assembly followed by ratification by a majority of P5 members.
A two-thirds vote in the UN General Assembly and ratification by all five permanent members.
Unanimous consent of the UN General Assembly and ratification by at least three permanent members.
A two-thirds vote in the UN Security Council and ratification by two-thirds of the UN General Assembly.

questions.explanation: Amending the UN Charter is a rigorous process. It requires a two-thirds majority vote in the UN General Assembly and must be ratified by two-thirds of the member states, including all five permanent members (P5) of the Security Council.
questions.takeaway: The veto power of the P5 extends to the amendment process of the UN Charter, acting as a significant structural barrier to reform.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Structural Barriers: Requires a UN Charter amendment, needing a two-thirds General Assembly vote and ratification by all five permanent members.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.prompt: The 'Uniting for Consensus' movement, frequently mentioned in the context of UN reforms, primarily aims to:

options.text: Abolish the veto power currently held by the five permanent members to democratize the council.
Support the G4 nations' bid for permanent seats to ensure better representation of the Global South.
Oppose the expansion of permanent membership in favor of increasing the number of non-permanent seats.
Ensure that at least two permanent seats are reserved for the African continent under the C10 proposal.

questions.explanation: The 'Uniting for Consensus' coalition, also known as the 'Coffee Club', specifically opposes the creation of new permanent seats. They generally favor expanding the non-permanent category to improve representation without creating new centers of permanent power.
questions.takeaway: The 'Coffee Club' is the primary diplomatic counter-weight to the G4's aspirations for permanent UNSC seats.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Opposition Groups: The 'Uniting for Consensus/Coffee Club' coalition opposes the creation of new permanent seats.
